name: ECA rule engine activation — parallel automation surface + JobOutbox pending-action queue
commandName: (none — no new chat command; this activates evaluateRules as a second postProcessor lane. A future admin-facing command/Mini-App page to author EcaRule rows is out of scope here; createEcaRuleGuarded is the hook point for it.)
repositoryFileContent:
See the file at packages/data/src/eca-rule-repository.ts (written directly). Summary: exports EcaRuleConditionOp/EcaRuleConditionRecord (structurally mirrors modules/automation/src/rule-engine.ts's RuleCondition without a package dependency — packages/data has no dep on modules/automation), EcaRuleRecord/EcaRulePatch, the EcaRuleRepository interface (create/list/update/remove/setEnabled/listActiveForEvent/recordFired), PrismaEcaRuleRepository (backed by a new `ecaRule` Prisma model — see prismaModelSnippet) and InMemoryEcaRuleRepository. `action` is typed as the EXISTING `AutomationAction` union imported from ./automation-repository.js — this repo does not invent a second action vocabulary. expiresAtMs/lastFiredMs are BigInt in Prisma (epoch ms, following the `lastEarnedMs BigInt` precedent already in schema.prisma) and are converted to/from `number` at the repository boundary so the pure rule-engine.ts (which works in plain numbers) never sees a bigint.

A second, equally new file was also written (the task's "pending-action queue" deliverable) at packages/data/src/job-outbox-repository.ts (+ job-outbox-repository.test.ts): JobOutboxRepository with enqueue/claim/complete/fail, backed by the ALREADY-EXISTING `JobOutbox` Prisma model (schema.prisma line ~552 — read, not redefined). Since that model has no `attempts` column, attempts/lastError are carried inside `payload` as an envelope `{ data, attempts, lastError? }`; `JobOutboxRecord.payload` unwraps it so callers only ever see their own business payload. `claim(topic, now, staleAfterMs, limit)` reclaims both due "pending" rows and stuck "processing" rows whose `lockedAt` is older than `staleAfterMs` (crash recovery) — documented as best-effort/non-SKIP-LOCKED, fine for this codebase's low-concurrency worker model.

Both files' InMemory classes are fully tested (20 tests total, all green via `node node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run packages/data/src/eca-rule-repository.test.ts packages/data/src/job-outbox-repository.test.ts`). `index.ts` got two new `export * from` lines appended at the very end (matching the file's existing tail convention of dated one-line comments before each new export), nothing else in index.ts was touched.

Typecheck (`node node_modules/typescript/bin/tsc -p packages/data/tsconfig.json --noEmit`): job-outbox-repository.ts is 100% clean (JobOutbox already exists in the generated Prisma client). eca-rule-repository.ts produces 7 `Property 'ecaRule' does not exist on type 'PrismaClient...'` errors — this is EXPECTED and matches the exact pre-existing pattern of the two other in-flight repository files in this same branch (admin-decision-repository.ts → 'adminDecision', known-admin-repository.ts → 'knownAdmin', both already committed as AM in git status): they all reference Prisma models that exist only as a snippet waiting to be added to schema.prisma + `prisma generate` by whichever process owns that file. I confirmed this is the established convention here (not a mistake) by running the same tsc command before my change and seeing those two files already failing identically. Once `prismaModelSnippet` below is added to schema.prisma and the client is regenerated, all 7 errors resolve with no code change needed.
prismaModelSnippet:
model EcaRule {
  id          String   @id @default(cuid())
  tenantId    String
  chatId      String?
  event       String
  conditions  Json
  action      Json
  enabled     Boolean  @default(true)
  cooldownMs  Int?
  expiresAtMs BigInt?
  lastFiredMs BigInt?
  createdAt   DateTime @default(now())
  updatedAt   DateTime @updatedAt

  @@index([tenantId])
  @@index([chatId])
  @@index([tenantId, event, enabled])
  @@map("eca_rules")
}
botUpdateServiceIntegrationNotes:
Read the real call site first (done): matchAndRunAutomations/OwnerNetworkAutomation lives at bot-update.service.ts lines 5952-5987 (list() call at 5971-5974, matchAutomation() at 5976); it is invoked from two postProcessor entries "automation-message"/"automation-new-members" at lines 1847-1858, which themselves call runAutomationsForMessage (5989-6005) / runAutomationsForNewMembers (6007-6018). None of that is touched — the new ECA path is a second, independent set of methods (matchAndRunEcaRules / runEcaRulesForMessage / runEcaRulesForNewMembers) added alongside, and two NEW postProcessor entries ("eca-rules-message", "eca-rules-new-members") appended right after the existing two, so both automation systems evaluate every event.

Exactly where each piece of the snippet goes (section numbers match the snippet's own `// N)` headers):
1. tokens.ts: append `ECA_RULE_REPOSITORY`/`JOB_OUTBOX_REPOSITORY` Symbols (I did not edit this file myself — it isn't in the forbidden list, but wasn't explicitly requested either, so I left it as a documented follow-up; it's a 2-line additive change, same risk class as the index.ts append I did make).
2. bot-update.service.ts: 4 import-block insertions (tokens.js; @superbot/data types; @superbot/data values; @superbot/module-automation) — all pure additions to existing named-import lists, alphabetically placed, no existing import removed or reordered beyond insertion.
3. Constructor: 2 new @Optional() params appended after the current last param `guardianRepository` (line 900-901), before `) {}` (line 902). Because they're optional-with-InMemory-default and appended at the very end, this needs ZERO changes to the ~35 `new BotUpdateService(...)` sites in bot-update.service.test.ts — confirmed this is the exact established pattern for every repo added in the last several sessions per this repo's own memory notes.
4. A single required behavior-preserving fix inside the EXISTING executeAutomationAction (its catch block at the very end, lines ~6119-6122): today it swallows every action failure with an empty `catch {}`, meaning no caller can ever detect failure — so retry-with-backoff on it is structurally impossible without this change. Changing it to rethrow is safe because its only current caller (matchAndRunAutomations, lines 5958-5987) already wraps the whole loop in its OWN try/catch with an identical "best-effort, never blocks" comment — so for the pre-existing simple-automation path, behavior is byte-for-byte unchanged (the throw is caught one frame up, silently, exactly as today). The new executeEcaActionWithRetry is the only caller that actually observes the throw.
5. New private methods: paste after the (now-fixed) executeAutomationAction, before routeEventForD1Kind (~line 6125). Includes: delay() (tiny sleep helper — none existed in the file), toEcaRuleFields() (AutomationEvent → RuleContext.fields), buildEcaRuleEdges()/assertNoEcaRuleLoop() (wires rule-loop-detector.ts's hasRuleLoop before a save), createEcaRuleGuarded() (the loop-guarded create entry point — nothing calls it yet since no command/Mini-App page authors EcaRule rows today; it's the hook for that future surface), executeEcaActionWithRetry() (wires retry-backoff.ts's computeBackoffMs/shouldRetry, falling back to JobOutbox.enqueue on exhaustion instead of dropping the action), matchAndRunEcaRules() (the evaluateRules() call site itself — the parallel counterpart of matchAndRunAutomations), and the two thin per-event wrappers runEcaRulesForMessage/runEcaRulesForNewMembers (parallel counterparts of runAutomationsForMessage/runAutomationsForNewMembers, same shape, same isServiceMessage guard).
6. botPostProcessors() array: 2 new entries appended right after "automation-new-members" (~line 1858), before the closing `];`.

Action-vocabulary reuse: executeAutomationAction (the SAME switch that already handles delete/reply/quarantine/notify_staff/log/mute/webhook/assign_mission for OwnerNetworkAutomation) is called as-is by executeEcaActionWithRetry — no second executor was built, per the instructions.

Rule-loop-detector scope note: EcaRule's action vocabulary has no explicit "trigger another rule" action, so a real trigger-loop can only arise structurally through a "reply" action re-emitting a "message" event that another (or the same) rule also listens for. buildEcaRuleEdges() models exactly that one conservative edge type; it deliberately does not attempt to model notify_staff/log (those post to a DIFFERENT chat — the staff chat — so they don't feed back into this chat's rule set) or assign_mission (not an AutomationEvent kind this engine listens for). This is a deliberately conservative design-level heuristic, not a full static-analysis proof.

JobOutbox / guardian-verify.service.ts TODO: I did not edit apps/api/src/guardian/guardian-verify.service.ts (not in my requested deliverables, and it's outside packages/data / apps/bot). Its TODO at line 692 ("route through JobOutbox for retry-with-backoff") can be closed with a follow-up one-liner: `await this.jobOutboxRepository.enqueue(tenantId, "guardian.staff-report.retry", { sessionId: session.id, ... })` inside that catch block, once a JobOutboxRepository is injected into GuardianVerifyService the same way it's injected into BotUpdateService above — left as a follow-up since guardian-verify.service.ts's constructor/DI shape wasn't part of this task's scope.
risks:
1. Prisma model not yet applied: eca-rule-repository.ts's Prisma-backed class references `this.client.ecaRule`, which does not typecheck until prismaModelSnippet is added to schema.prisma and `prisma generate` is re-run — I verified this is the SAME pre-existing state as the other two in-flight repository files on this branch (admin-decision-repository.ts/'adminDecision', known-admin-repository.ts/'knownAdmin'), confirmed by running the typecheck before touching anything. InMemoryEcaRuleRepository has no such dependency and is fully tested (11 tests green).

2. The one required change to executeAutomationAction's catch block (empty catch → rethrow) is the one place this design touches EXISTING behavior of the forbidden-to-edit file's logic, even though it's expressed only as a snippet+instructions here, not applied directly. It is deliberately minimal and I verified by reading matchAndRunAutomations that its outer try/catch fully absorbs the rethrow with identical end-user behavior for the pre-existing path — but whoever applies this snippet should double check no OTHER caller of executeAutomationAction exists beside matchAndRunAutomations (I grepped: it is the only call site as of this read).

3. rule-loop-detection (assertNoEcaRuleLoop/createEcaRuleGuarded) has no caller yet because no chat-command or Mini-App page exists to let an admin author EcaRule rows — this task only asked to design the activation of evaluateRules against ALREADY-PERSISTED rules, not to build that authoring surface, so createEcaRuleGuarded is inert until such a surface calls it. Rules can still be seeded directly via the repository (e.g. from a seed script or a future controller) but will bypass the loop guard if they call `ecaRuleRepository.create(...)` directly instead of `createEcaRuleGuarded(...)` — worth a lint/code-review note for whoever builds that surface.

4. JobOutboxRepository has no worker-side drain loop yet (nothing calls `claim()` on topic "eca.action.retry" or processes it) — enqueue() is wired so failed ECA actions are never silently dropped, but they will sit in `job_outbox` as "pending" until a worker job (apps/worker) is added to poll and retry them. That worker job is a natural next step but wasn't requested here.

5. tokens.ts and apps/bot/src/app.module.ts (DI provider registration for ECA_RULE_REPOSITORY/JOB_OUTBOX_REPOSITORY) were intentionally left as documented follow-ups rather than edited directly — without that registration, NestJS will inject `undefined` for those two params and the JS default parameters (InMemoryEcaRuleRepository/InMemoryJobOutboxRepository) will silently take over in production too, meaning ECA rules and outbox jobs would NOT actually persist to Postgres until app.module.ts registers real Prisma-backed providers. This mirrors the exact same "default kicks in until DI is wired" behavior already accepted for every other optional repo in this constructor, so it's consistent, but flagging it since it means "activation" here is code-complete but not yet live end-to-end without that last DI step.
